missing_points.point: Contract formation: was there a bargain?
missing_points.reason: No final rubric directly covers the extracted application.

R002: conditional gift: The harder formation question is whether the marriage is bargained-for consideration (the “price” of the father’s promise) or merely a condition on a gratuitous promise (a conditional gift).
But classic suretyship Statute of Frauds analysis typically turns on whether the promise is made to the creditor to answer for another’s debt.
The father’s hoped-for tax deduction is a pecuniary motive, but courts vary on whether that is “direct” enough.
If the father had separately promised the lender to pay or guarantee the loans, that would be within suretyship and ordinarily require a writing (subject to exceptions like “main purpose”).

Conclusion The father’s oral promise is likely unenforceable because it is a promise made in consideration of marriage, which falls within the Statute of Frauds and requires a signed writing, and none exists.

Statute of Frauds “gates” (enforceability, not proof) Gate A: Contract in consideration of marriage (dispositive here*) Under the traditional Statute of Frauds, a promise is unenforceable unless in a signed writing when it is a contract made upon consideration of marriage, other than mutual promises to marry.
R003: If the father had separately promised the lender to pay or guarantee the loans, that would be within suretyship and ordinarily require a writing (subject to exceptions like “main purpose”).
Even where estoppel is allowed, remedies may be limited to what justice requires rather than full expectation.

Conclusion The father’s oral promise is likely unenforceable because it is a promise made in consideration of marriage, which falls within the Statute of Frauds and requires a signed writing, and none exists.

Statute of Frauds “gates” (enforceability, not proof) Gate A: Contract in consideration of marriage (dispositive here*) Under the traditional Statute of Frauds, a promise is unenforceable unless in a signed writing when it is a contract made upon consideration of marriage, other than mutual promises to marry.

The son’s full performance (the marriage) does not automatically remove this category from the Statute of Frauds; the writing requirement still applies unless the jurisdiction recognizes a specific substitute doctrine (discussed below).

Even where estoppel is allowed, remedies may be limited to what justice requires rather than full expectation.

But classic suretyship Statute of Frauds analysis typically turns on whether the promise is made to the creditor to answer for another’s debt.

Statute of Frauds “gates” (enforceability, not proof) Gate A: Contract in consideration of marriage (dispositive here*) Under the traditional Statute of Frauds, a promise is unenforceable unless in a signed writing when it is a contract made upon consideration of marriage, other than mutual promises to marry.

The son’s full performance (the marriage) does not automatically remove this category from the Statute of Frauds; the writing requirement still applies unless the jurisdiction recognizes a specific substitute doctrine (discussed below).

Exceptions/substitutes “Main purpose”/“leading object” doctrine (only relevant if suretyship applies) If a suretyship writing requirement were triggered (e.g., promise to creditor), the “main purpose” exception may remove the promise from that suretyship category if the father’s dominant purpose was to secure a personal economic benefit.
R013: Fact cutting against bargain (and against reliance later): the son “agreed because he already planned to propose,” which suggests the promise may not have induced the marriage.
On these facts, the promise is made to the son (the debtor), not to the lender.
Promissory estoppel (possible but weak on these facts; often limited against SoF) The son might argue promissory estoppel to enforce an oral promise to avoid injustice.

R016: Fact cutting against bargain (and against reliance later): the son “agreed because he already planned to propose,” which suggests the promise may not have induced the marriage.
Gate C: Suretyship (promise to pay another’s debt; secondary and nuanced) There is also a potential suretyship issue because the father promised to pay an existing debt (student loans).
On these facts, the promise is made to the son (the debtor), not to the lender.
Promissory estoppel (possible but weak on these facts; often limited against SoF) The son might argue promissory estoppel to enforce an oral promise to avoid injustice.
